Numerical studies confirm the effectiveness of proposed approach in MRE utilization, cost-efficiency, and decarbonization capabilities for the OPLS.

item.title: Reactive Power Coordination Control to Enhance Weak Grid Stability of Hybrid Power Plants
item.description: The rise of inverter-based resources (IBRs) and the retirement of synchronous generators weaken modern power systems by lowering the system strength. This leads to a lower short-circuit ratio ($SCR$) at the point of common coupling (PCC) of IBR facilities, resulting in weak grid conditions that hinder power transfer and threaten angle stability. To prevent angle instability, maintaining the PCC voltage at $1\,\text{pu}$ or higher is crucial, but injecting high active power into weak grids often increases the PCC voltage due to the high coupling impedance. Conventional voltage droop control ($QV-$droop) supplies reactive power by reducing the voltage at PCC, and compromises angle stability and contradicts the voltage requirement in weak grids. Hybrid power plants (HPPs), which combine grid-forming (GFM) IBRs (like energy storage) and grid-following (GFL) IBRs (like wind/solar), can enhance angle stability and ensure power transfer in weak grids. This study presents a dynamic reactive power sharing strategy at the plant level that uses GFM IBRs for PCC voltage control and GFL IBRs for reactive power support. The proposed algorithm was validated through EMT Simulations, demonstrating its efficacy under weak grid conditions.

item.title: A Weight-Allocation and Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning Approach for the BTM PV Power Forecasting
item.description: Distributed ultra-short-term photovoltaic (PV) power forecasting plays a vital role in analyzing behind-the-meter (BTM) solar generation and enhancing grid stability. As federated learning (FL) is increasingly applied to distributed BTM PV forecasting on a large scale, its deployment is confronted with two major challenges, namely performance degradation arising from device heterogeneity and privacy risks associated with global communication. To address these challenges, a novel collaborative method, termed AWA-DDP-FL, is proposed, which integrates adaptive weight assignment (AWA) with reinforcement learning-driven dynamic differential privacy (DDP). Specifically, AWA mitigates the impact of device heterogeneity by strategically excluding underperforming clients and dynamically adjusting local model aggregation weights during training. Meanwhile, DDP enables each distributed PV client to autonomously adjust its privacy budget in real time according to data sensitivity, balancing privacy protection and model performance through dynamic noise injection. Experimental results on real-world PV datasets show that the proposed method obtains normalized root mean square error of 5.110% and normalized mean absolute error of 2.630%, indicating that the proposed algorithm achieves higher accuracy even under noise perturbations.

item.title: Causal Self-Supervised Graph Learning for Robust PV Power Forecasting Against Distribution Shift
item.description: Due to stochastic weather changes and the increasing frequency of extreme weather events in recent years, photovoltaic (PV) power generation data frequently undergo probability distribution shifts in practice. This poses huge challenges for traditional machine learning-based power forecasting methods that rely on unstable correlations between input and output for predictions. To this end, this paper proposes a causal self-supervised graph learning framework for robust power forecasting of multiple PV sites. Specifically, a structural causal model is first built to describe the PV power generation process, which allows us to distinguish the confounder that causes the failure of prior studies. Then, the spurious correlations caused by the confounder are removed through causality intervention with do-calculus and back-door adjustment, which is further solved by our proposed theoretical solution, termed PV generation environment disentangled adaptation (PV-EDA). After that, a self-supervised multi-view graph neural network is devised to implement PV-EDA in a fidelity way, such that causality intervention is effectively realized within the designed forecasting model. The stable causality improves the generalization ability of the proposed model and allows it to achieve robust forecasting performance under varying distributional shift scenarios. Numerical experiments are conducted on real-world PV power output data to evaluate the model’s day-ahead forecasting performance. The results show that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art PV forecasting approaches under various distribution shift scenarios.

item.title: A Heterogeneous Graph Spatiotemporal Model for Distributed PV Cluster Forecasting Under Adaptive Partition Collaborative Framework
item.description: As the penetration rate of distributed photovoltaics (PV) continues to rise, accurate forecasting of distributed PV clusters becomes critical for ensuring the economic operation of power systems. However, the grid interconnection of numerous distributed PV stations imposes exponential computational resource demands. To address these challenges, we propose a short-term cluster forecasting model for distributed PV based on heterogeneous spatiotemporal learning under adaptive partition collaborative framework. The spatial model constructs multi-dimensional heterogeneous subgraphs integrating geographical locations, meteorological correlations, and historical PV power data to capture complex spatial dependencies among distributed PV stations. The adaptive partition framework dynamically divides PV clusters and trains sub-models by a weighted clustering algorithm to maintain accuracy and efficiency. Moreover, the inference module of proposed model can be implemented on Raspberry Pi-based edge device to simulate real user-side operating environments. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed heterogeneous graph spatiotemporal model under adaptive partition collaborative framework achieves 4% higher forecasting accuracy, 30% faster training, and 25% lower inference latency compared to centralized baselines. The adaptive partition collaborative framework outperforms the completed centralized one in both computational efficiency and forecasting accuracy.

item.title: Stability Region Analysis for HVDC-Connected Offshore Wind Farm Participating in Grid Frequency Support
item.description: When the high-voltage direct current-connected offshore wind farm (HVDC-connected OWF) participates in grid frequency support, excessively high frequency controller gains may exacerbate the electromagnetic oscillations within system. Therefore, it is essential to study the stability region for frequency controller gains of HVDC-connected OWF, thus ensuring the grid secure operation during frequency events. Currently, numerical methods are used to analyze the operational stability region of HVDC-connected OWF. However, these methods involve exhaustive calculations to obtain the stability region, resulting in a significant computational burden. To address the issue, this work proposes an analytical stability region identification method for HVDC-connected OWF when it participates in the grid frequency support. Specifically, a comprehensive small signal model, tailored for analyzing frequency support-induced oscillation, is first developed by considering both the frequency regulation characteristics and the power converter dynamics. Further, using the Padé approximation technique, the reduced order equivalent model is derived while preserving its accuracy. With this equivalent model, the mechanism underlying frequency support-induced oscillation is qualitatively elucidated, and the stability region for frequency support is also analytically derived. Finally, the analysis results are validated through electromagnetic transient simulations.

item.title: Online Monitoring of Batteries in Modular Multilevel Energy Storage Systems Without Disturbing the Electrical Network
item.description: Accurate monitoring of battery parameters is essential for optimizing the performance, safety and longevity of grid-scale battery energy storage systems (BESSs). Conventionally, equivalent circuit model (ECM) parameter identification is performed through offline testing, which results in costly downtime, or through online perturbation-based methods, which disturb the grid undesirably. In contrast, this paper proposes a method for online ECM parameter identification in modular multilevel cascaded converter (MMCC)-based BESSs that introduces no disturbances into the electrical grid. The proposed technique injects controlled current harmonic perturbations into the battery terminals while using a novel control strategy to cancel the net effect of these harmonics at the grid interface. This is enabled by leveraging the inherent interleaving capability of the MMCC-based BESS. This control-based solution eliminates the need for oversized buffer capacitors or extra hardware, and can be integrated seamlessly within existing control schemes. Validation is performed using a 3.84-kW/3.46-kWh lab prototype under various grid conditions, demonstrating accurate real-time ECM parameter estimation via recursive least squares without distorting the grid current.

item.title: Distributed Large-Signal Stability Analysis and Parameter Optimization of Offshore Wind Power Transmission Systems
item.description: The volatility of wind speeds and the random nature of faults require offshore wind power transmission systems to maintain stable operation under large disturbance conditions. Considering the different equilibrium points of wind turbines and the bidirectional power flow of rectifier stations, the dynamic analysis of the entire offshore AC system is extremely complex, posing challenges for large-signal stability control. To address this issue, this paper proposes a distributed large-signal stability optimization method under the framework of passive control, models offshore wind turbines and rectifier stations with mixed potential functions, and ultimately derives the key constraints that strongly affect the entire offshore AC system via Brayton-Moser criterion. Comparing with the classic method, the proposed method rectifies the unreasonable expansion of the stable region caused by the power balance calculation, and points out the contribution of zero-sequence control in modular multilevel converters to the stable operation under unexpected power change conditions, which are still effective in expanded wind power plants and time-varying transmission line parameters. Finally, the correctness of the theoretical analysis and parameter settings is verified by simulation results.

item.title: Bridging Market Efficiency and System Resilience: Unified Multi-Service Operation of Long-Duration Energy Storage in Decarbonized Power Systems
item.description: The rapid growth of variable renewable generation and the increasing frequency of extreme events demand power systems that are both economical and resilient. Long-duration energy storage (LDES) stands out as a unique solution, capable of shifting energy across multiple days while providing critical support during large-scale outages. However, our review of existing approaches highlights critical gaps: market operations and system restoration are typically optimized in isolation, and the technology-specific characteristics of LDES make their coordinated use especially challenging. We first investigate these challenges, including efficiency-capacity trade-offs, duration mismatches, and restoration-sequence dependencies, and underscore the need for an integrated framework. To address this gap, this paper introduces a unified multi-service optimization model that co-optimizes LDES for both economic and resilience objectives within a single mixed-integer linear program. The formulation combines energy arbitrage, capacity adequacy, and renewable integration with resilience metrics based on avoided unserved energy and a priority-based restoration mechanism capturing spatiotemporal re-energization and progressive load pickup. Multi-objective Pareto analysis reveals cost-resilience trade-offs, providing actionable insights for system planners. To demonstrate technology-agnostic applicability, the model is applied to two contrasting LDES technologies, hydrogen (H$_{2}$) and flow battery (FB), on a benchmark test system with seasonal PV and load profiles under diverse blackout scenarios. Simulation results show that the proposed framework reduces operating costs in normal conditions while accelerating critical-load recovery and system-wide restoration after disruptions, advancing the state-of-the-art in storage-integrated power system operation.

item.title: Secure Operation of a Multi-Energy Microgrid via Coordinated Preventive and Corrective Control Under Diverse Uncertainties
item.description: The operation of multi-energy microgrid (MEMG) is threatened by random failures and uncertainties in renewable energy sources (RESs) and loads. This paper proposes a systematic approach for secure operation of MEMG by coordinating preventive control (PC) and corrective control (CC) under diverse uncertainties. First, a day-ahead three-stage stochastic PC-CC coordinated optimization model is proposed to determine day-ahead PC measures while considering CC to address potential contingencies. Then, as uncertainties gradually unfold, an intra-day rolling PC-CC coordinated optimization model is developed to iteratively obtain intra-day PC measures accounting for potential near-term contingencies. When a contingency really occurs, the CC optimization is activated to determine CC measures. Furthermore, a contingency probability model that considers weather conditions and component aging is established. Contingency impact is quantified and embedded in optimization models. Third, a two-step sampling method is proposed for scenario generation, capturing uncertainties and temporal correlation of RESs, loads and failures. A scenario-based decomposition method employing the progressive hedging (PH) algorithm is developed to solve the day-ahead model. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach reduces security control costs by 34.92%-45.90% over methods limited to PC or CC alone and improves computation efficiency by 2.20 times.

item.title: Cost-Emissions-Efficiency Tradeoff Analysis for Urban District Energy Systems With Distributed Energy Resources
item.description: The high population density in cities makes urban energy systems critical to the sustainable energy transition. District energy systems (DESs) with combined heat and power (CHP) plants are particularly important, as these long-term infrastructure investments form a foundation for this energy transition. Integrating distributed energy resources (DERs) into DESs can supercharge local and efficient production of electricity, heating, and cooling – achieving further cost, emissions, and fuel efficiency targets. While the promising impact of adopting DERs has been studied, a method to identify how each DER improves the ability to meet multiple objectives simultaneously remains lacking. This paper formulates an energy dispatch model of a DES with a CHP plant that can integrate battery and thermal storage, solar photovoltaics, solar water heaters, and absorption chillers. We provide a meta-heuristic solution framework to identify the Pareto-candidate solution set for cost, emissions, and CHP plant efficiency that this DES can achieve. We then apply Pareto tradeoff theory to identify the optimal dispatch schedule that meets multiple objectives simultaneously and quantify how the schedule improves through the inclusion of DERs. We also quantify how a DER installation can reduce tradeoffs between objectives, so the optimal cost, emission, and fuel efficiency schedules are more alike. The proposed analysis framework is tested on two DESs in Washington, D.C., which differ in scale and building types. Additionally, we show how the presence of a carbon tax can alter the impact that DERs have on the tradeoff analysis. This work offers a methodology that helps energy managers select the best DER installations that would meet multi-dimensional targets. Moreover, policymakers can understand which existing systems must sacrifice emission and fuel efficiency for cost, then strategically incentivize a multi-faceted energy transition.

item.title: Multi-Rate Fast Frequency Regulation With High-Fidelity Neural Network Model of the Electrolyzer Process
item.description: Frequency regulation is crucial for grid stability, especially with the increasing penetration of inverter-based resources (IBRs). Hydrogen electrolyzers, as flexible loads, offer a promising solution by dynamically adjusting power consumption to counteract frequency deviations. While existing studies primarily focus on electrolyzer stacks and power converters, the role of auxiliary process components such as pumps and coolers remains largely unexplored. This paper proposes a multi-rate frequency response (MRFR) model for hydrogen electrolyzer process plants, distinguishing frequency regulation contributions from both the electrolyzer stack and auxiliary components. A high-fidelity neural network (HiFiNN) model is developed using a detailed alkaline water electrolysis (AWE) process model in Aspen HYSYS, accurately capturing the interactions between electrical and thermodynamic parameters. The HiFiNN model is integrated with a model predictive controller (MPC) to optimize frequency regulation while maintaining process stability. The proposed framework is validated through simulations and real-time controller-hardware-in-the-loop (CHIL) experiment to validate its feasibility for practical deployment. Additionally, its effectiveness is demonstrated under varying IBR penetration levels, highlighting its capability to maintain frequency stability in low-inertia power systems.

item.title: Decision-Oriented Dynamic Operating Envelopes for Distributed Energy Resources via End-to-End Learning With Simulation-in-the-Loop
item.description: The large-scale exploitation of distributed energy resources (DERs) has significantly enhanced the sustainability, flexibility, and cost efficiency of distribution networks. Dynamic operating envelopes (DOEs) provide a technically reliable and practical solution to ensure operational security and facilitate DER utilization. This paper proposes an end-to-end learning-based approach with simulation-in-the-loop (E2E-SiL) for optimizing the DOEs for DERs, which merits a decision-oriented training framework with excellent reliability and computational efficiency. An input convex neural network (ICNN) model is applied to estimate the mapping relationship from bus power injection to constraint violation while ensuring the convexity of analytical expressions for critical physical quantities in DOE optimization. Based on the ICNN model, the DOE optimization problem is transformed into a fully linear form solvable within seconds. To bridge the gap between theoretical DOE formulation and practical applications, simulation software is utilized to evaluate the actual loss and guide the ICNN parameter updates, formulating a simulation-in-the-loop structure and enhancing the practicality of DOE results. A backpropagation algorithm is proposed to derive parameter gradients theoretically, which achieves efficient training and better physical interpretability. Numerical experiments validate the superiority of the proposed E2E-SiL framework, which enhances the practicality of DOE results and ensures better utilization of DERs.

item.title: Cooperative Planning for Integrated Electric Heat System Considering Multi-Dimensional Operation Risks and Benefit/Cost Ratio
item.description: In order to enhance the operation economics and reduce the operation risks of large-scale integrated electric heat system (IEHS), as well as to generate the planning scheme with maximum benefits for each unit of investment, a cooperative planning method for IEHS considering multi-dimensional operation risks and benefit/cost ratio (BCR) is proposed. First, a district heat system (DHS) segmented virtual heat storage model considering regulation capacity quantification is developed, which can accurately quantify the electric power regulation capacity of DHS while considering heat storage characteristics. Then, a cooperative planning model for IEHS considering multi-dimensional operation risks and BCR is constructed, where BCR indicators considering the benefits of reducing operation cost and multi-dimensional risks including adequacy, flexibility and security are designed. Next, for the planning model containing fractional objective caused by BCR indicators, a solution method based on McCormick envelope and dynamic step sequential bound tightening is proposed for effectively enhancing the solution accuracy. Finally, an IEHS containing a 12-node 500kV transmission power system and two 10-node DHSs is used for case studies. Simulation results indicate that the proposed method outperforms other existing methods in terms of increasing BCR indicators of planning scheme, utilizing heat storage characteristics and electric power regulation capacity of DHS, and enhancing solution accuracy. Compared with the state before planning, the proposed method reduces the adequacy, flexibility, and security risks of IEHS by 72.68%, 62.11%, 49.99% respectively while reducing the operation cost by 12.39%.

item.title: A Fault-Tolerant Power Processing Architecture for Resilient LVDC Networks With Integrated Photovoltaics
item.description: This paper proposes a novel fault-tolerant power processing architecture for photovoltaic (PV) systems within low-voltage direct current (LVDC) networks, aimed at enhancing operational resilience and reliability in sustainable energy systems. The architecture integrates a bidirectional dc/dc converter for maximumpower point tracking (MPPT) and a buck converter to supply a stable 24 V output from a 48 V LVDC bus, ensuring seamless operation for distributed consumer loads under varying environmental and fault conditions. Fault-tolerance is achieved through built-in redundancy and adaptive control mechanisms that support continuous operation during converter failures. The proposed system's performance is evaluated through comprehensive case studies and real-time hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing using the OPAL-RT platform. Results demonstrate the system's effectiveness in maintaining energy continuity and voltage stability, highlighting its potential for deployment in resilient, PV-integrated LVDC networks supporting sustainable energy goals.

item.title: ANTDCC: A Lightweight and Generalizable Framework for Long-Term Photovoltaic Power Forecasting via Adaptive Normalization and Dynamic Channel Clustering
item.description: The intermittency and uncertainty of photovoltaic (PV) power generation have posed significant challenges to grid stability and efficient integration of renewable energy, especially in long-term forecasting tasks where accurately modeling multi-channel temporal dependencies and adapting to dynamic distribution shifts have become critical issues. To address the limitations of existing methods in channel modeling strategies and normalization mechanisms, this paper has proposed an efficient PV power forecasting framework—ANTDCC—that integrates adaptive normalization, dynamic channel clustering, and TSMixer. First, an Attention-alike Structural Re-parameterization Dynamic tanh (ASRDyT) has been introduced to enhance the model adaptability to temporal distribution changes. Second, TSMixer has been employed as the backbone network, leveraging its linear computational complexity and efficient time-feature mixing capability to effectively capture global temporal dependencies while balancing accuracy and efficiency. Third, a Dynamic Channel Clustering Mechanism (DCCM) has been designed to excavate inter-channel synergies, improving model interpretability and parameter utilization. Finally, systematic evaluations on PV datasets from Australia and China have demonstrated that the proposed method has significantly outperformed baseline models across four forecasting horizons, exhibiting superior generalization ability, thereby providing effective support for intelligent PV plant scheduling and optimized power system operation.

item.title: Preference-Oriented Evolutionary Multi-Objective Energy-Noise Optimization for Fast Wind Farm Layout Design
item.description: Mitigating wind farm noise through layout optimization while maintaining power production under varying noise limits remains challenging. To reduce workload and decision time, and engage DMs more in optimization, this study proposes a preference-oriented evolutionary multi-objective (EMO) framework for wind farm layout optimization that incorporates decision-makers’ (DMs’) preferences through interactively updated reference points. The framework guides the search toward DMs’ region of interest effectively while preserving population diversity. Case studies on a real-world wind farm show that the proposed algorithm outperforms four state-of-the-art EMO algorithms and a traditional layout method. Compared with the real-world layouts, the resulting solutions reduce the noise level by 10.45% –23.04% and increase the annual energy production by 3.03% –4.09%, while aligning with DMs’ preferences.

item.title: Almost Globally Stable GFL Inverter Control Scheme Based on Current Control Synchronization
item.description: Various control strategies have been proposed to enhance the transient stability of grid-following (GFL) inverters. However, most existing approaches are tailored to specific operating conditions and often lack theoretical guarantees, limiting their adaptability. Furthermore, these methods tend to complicate the controller design and parameter tuning. To address these limitations, this paper introduces a novel current control synchronization (CCS)-based control strategy for GFL inverters. The stability of the CCS-based control scheme is rigorously verified using the qualitative theory of differential equations and phase portrait analysis, establishing almost global asymptotic stability. Moreover, the controller parameters can be tuned with exceptional simplicity, greatly streamlining the design process. The performance of the CCS-based approach is validated through electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations and controller hardware-in-the-loop (CHIL) experiments conducted on the StarSim platform. The results show that the proposed method matches phase-locked loop-based schemes in power tracking and frequency synchronization, while significantly outperforming them in transient stability. These findings further underscore the enhanced adaptability and practical feasibility of the proposed method.

item.title: Quantitative Parameter Conditions for Stability and Coupling in GFM–GFL Converter Hybrid Systems From a Small-Signal Synchronous Perspective
item.description: With the development of renewable energy sources, power systems are gradually evolving into a system comprising both grid-forming (GFM) and grid-following (GFL) converters. However, the dynamic interactions between the two types of converters remain unclear, particularly in the presence of low-inertia GFM converters, due to the fundamental differences in their synchronization mechanisms. To address this gap, this paper develops a small-signal synchronous stability model for power systems containing GFM and GFL converters, which considers network line dynamics. Based on matrix perturbation, we reveal that GFM and GFL subsystems can be effectively decoupled when GFL converters operate near unity power factor or when GFM converters possess sufficiently large inertia or damping, and provide lower bound of control parameters ensuring decoupling. Under the decoupling condition, we propose decentralized and analytical parameter-based stability criteria which have clear physical interpretations: the positive damping of converters compensates for the negative damping of the network. In the case of coupling, we also propose decentralized stability criteria based on the small phase theorem. The effectiveness of the theoretical analysis is validated through simulations in MATLAB/Simulink.

item.title: CDT-RiskNet: A Risk-Aware Copula-Diffusion-Transformer Network for Stochastic EV Charging Station Optimization in Energy Market
item.description: The growing penetration of photovoltaic (PV) systems and electric vehicles (EVs) poses systemic uncertainty for EV charging stations (EVCSs) participating in day-ahead energy market. These uncertainties are characterized by complex dependencies and time-varying tail risks that challenge traditional scenario generation and risk management techniques. This paper presents CDT-RiskNet, a Copula-Diffusion-Transformer framework for risk-aware stochastic optimization. A copula-enhanced diffusion model is developed to generate realistic joint scenarios of PV generation and EV charging demand. To manage time-varying tail risk, a Transformer-based risk module predicts dynamic CVaR weights from both historical and forecasted features, enabling coordinated evaluation across time. Simulation resultson both small-scale and large-scale EVCSs using real-world data demonstrate that CDT-RiskNet improves scenario generation quality, risk control, and adaptability to varying market conditions, leading to better economic performance under uncertainty.

item.title: Enhancing Converter-Driven Stability of Solar-PV Grid-Supporting Inverters Under Weak Grid Conditions
item.description: Solar photovoltaic (PV)-driven grid-following inverters (GFLI) exhibit stable operation in a strong grid environment but are prone to limited fault ride-through capability, increased oscillations under disturbances, and even instability under weak grid conditions. In the conventional GFLI control strategy, one contributing factor to these limitations is the low stability margin of the PI-based active/reactive power (PQ) controller, particularly under weak-grid conditions. This study addresses these issues by developing an integral terminal sliding mode control (ITSMC)-based PQ controller for the secondary control layer of the grid-supporting GFLI. Unlike traditional linear controllers, the proposed controller offers enhanced robustness under weak grids, finite-time convergence, and inherent post-fault oscillation damping (PFOD) capabilities without additional damping strategies. Furthermore, a simplified small-signal model that incorporates both the primary and secondary control layers, together with grid impedance, is developed to analyze inverter stability under varying grid strengths for both conventional and proposed methods. To enable grid supporting functions, an online short circuit ratio (SCR)-based adaptive method is introduced, ensuring robust performance under extremely weak grids without compromising grid code standards. The proposed method is validated through a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) experiment and compared with conventional and existing power oscillation damping (POD) methods. Subsequently, a case study is conducted with a multi-inverter IEEE 33-bus distribution network that includes real-world contingency events, demonstrating enhanced stability and compliance with grid-code requirements.

item.title: Integrated Driving Method to Optimize Operation of Wind Turbines for Weakening the Wake Effect
item.description: The wake effect in wind farms (WFs) leads to wind energy loss and increases the fatigue load of wind turbines (WTs). In this paper, an integrated driving method (IDM) to optimize operation of wind turbines (WTs) for weakening the wake effect. Considering the wake effect with delayed time, a model predicted control (MPC)-based WT control method is proposed to optimize the dual-parameter, i.e., pitch angle and generator torque, for mitigating fatigue load and increasing power generation. To pursue the better optimization effect, the data-driven method (DDM) is proposed to learn the accurate and complete dataset for eliminating linearization errors in the nonlinear physical of wind turbines and engineering wake models. DDM is designed to capture the nonlinear aerodynamic interactions based on the operational status of WTs in wake-affected WFs. It utilizes a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based encoder and a graph neural network (GNN)-based decoder. Subsequently, an artificial neural network (ANN)-based fusion method is proposed to select the better optimization values of these two methods for improving the prediction reliability and anti-measurement noise interference ability. Finally, the efficacy of the proposed scheme is substantiated through case studies involving a wake-affected WF.

item.title: Coordinated Fuzzy Active Power Control of Wind Turbines With Load Optimization
item.description: In modern power systems with a high penetration of wind energy, active power control (APC) enables wind turbines to track the power reference set by the control center. However, the rapidity and accuracy requirements of APC mode always lead to large fatigue loads of turbines’ components. This paper proposes a set of fuzzy logic-based APC strategies integrated together to achieve the joint load optimization of key components while reducing power loss. Firstly, a lower bound parameter for pitch range limitation updated by rotor speed is created to realize a dynamic adjustment of the variable-speed range participating in active power tracking, effectively reducing the load of the pitch system. Secondly, a torque acceleration adjustment parameter is updated adaptively through the established fuzzy logic rules to optimize both acceleration and deceleration performance, thereby reducing power production loss when the available wind power is less than the desired reference. Furthermore, we design an upper bound parameter for pitch range limitation to find a load optimization trade-off among the pitch system, the drive train, and the blade root under high wind speed. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed controllers is validated on a semi-physical hardware testing platform.

item.title: Frequency Response Model and Inertia Enhancement Method of Islanded Microgrid Integrated With Free Piston Stirling Generator
item.description: Dish-Stirling solar-thermal generating is a promising technology for sustainable development. It has attracted wide attention due to its high efficiency, cogeneration and peak shaving performance. As the power conversion unit, the free piston Stirling generator (FPSG) has excellent inertial support and overcurrent capacity, which makes it have good application prospects in islanded microgrid (MG). However, since FPSG is a multi-physics coupling system of thermal, mechanical and electrical fields, its high-order nonlinear characteristics lead to significant difficulties in frequency response analysis. In order to fill this gap, this paper linearizes the Schmidt model and then derives the frequency response model of FPSG by analogy with the mechanical swing equation of the synchronous generator (SG). Subsequently, the time domain analysis method was used to find that it exhibits over-damping characteristics, which confirms its inertial support capacity from a theoretical level. Then, considering the different characteristics of FPSG and energy storage system (ESS), a self-adaptive inertia enhancement control method is proposed, which uses FPSG to provide long-term high-power support whereas ESS tackles short-term power compensation. Finally, the feasibility of the proposed concept is validated through simulation and experimental results, which indicate that 48.84% and 54.89% improvements of the frequency nadir and rate of change of frequency (RoCoF) can be achieved.

item.title: Deriving the Robust Operating Region for Multistage Dispatch of Power Systems: A Decision-Dependent Uncertainties Based Modeling Approach
item.description: The flexibility of energy storage systems (ESS) is essential for mitigating the uncertainties associated with variable renewable energy (VRE) sources in power system operations. While ESS scheduling for high-renewable systems has been widely studied, accurately coordinating energy-limited ESS with intertemporal coupling under uncertainty and non-anticipative operation remains a challenging robust scheduling task in practice. In this context, a multistage robust scheduling model is developed that captures the energy-limited characteristics of ESS while enforcing non-anticipativity. The model schedules upper and lower boundaries of ESS state of energy (SOE) and generator outputs in the day-ahead timeframe and derives a robust operating region (OR) via decision-dependent uncertainty (DDU). These robust ORs enable temporal decoupling so that the multistage problem admits an equivalent, tractable two-stage formulation. A parallel Nested-C&CG algorithm is employed to compute the optimal robust OR (OR$^{\mathrm{2}}$) efficiently. In addition, an enhanced look-ahead intra-day dispatch is introduced that dynamically updates a window-specific robust OR using ultra–short-term VRE forecasts and their uncertainty, while anchoring the window end to the day-ahead OR$^{\mathrm{2}}$ to preserve recursive feasibility; this mechanism reduces day-ahead conservatism and maintains robustness across the rolling horizon. Numerical experiments conducted on modified IEEE 6-bus and IEEE 118-bus systems demonstrate the model's advantages in robustness, economic efficiency, and tractability.

item.title: Fast Active Power Reduction Based Fault Ride-Through Control Strategy for VSC-HVDC Integrating Offshore Wind Farm Under Onshore Fault
item.description: The voltage source converter-based high voltage direct current (VSC-HVDC) integrating offshore wind farm (OWF) system suffers from direct current (DC) overvoltage under onshore grid fault. The existing methods mainly use DC chopper of VSC-HVDC or voltage drop control of the OWF to mitigate DC overvoltage, which lead to high costs or forced tripping of offshore wind turbine generators (WTGs). To suppress DC overvoltage and promote active power recovery, this paper proposes a two-stage fast active power reduction (FAPR) based fault ride-through control strategy. By mathematically clarifying the FAPR capacity of the WTG, the DC chopper resistances of the WTG and VSC-HVDC are economically designed. Then, during stage 1, a scheme that combines FAPR control of the OWF with DC chopper control of VSC-HVDC is proposed to suppresses DC overvoltage. During stage 2, considering the issue of electromagnetic torque saturation when offshore WTGs undergo fast active power recovery, a fast pitch angle reduction control scheme is proposed to facilitate the active power recovery. Simulations and experiments validate that the proposed method avoids DC overvoltage. Besides, the DC chopper resistors are reduced by 90% and 80% compared with DC chopper-based method and voltage drop-based method, respectively.

item.title: Medium-Frequency Oscillation Analysis and Single-Parameter Feedback Suppression for Full-Size Converter-Based Variable-Speed Pumped Storage and Renewable Energy Combined System
item.description: The coordinated operation of the full-size converter-based variable-speed pumped storage unit (FSC-VSPSU) and renewable energy station can help smooth the power fluctuations and improve the renewable energy utilization. However, this combined system may encounter instability problem such as medium-frequency oscillation in practical application. The impedance-based analysis method is commonly used for addressing such instability problems. Nevertheless, existing research lacks an impedance model of FSC-VSPSU and does not fully conduct the stability analysis of the system. Hence, this paper develops the impedance model for FSC-VSPSU. By integrating it with the impedance model of renewable energy units, an equivalent impedance model of the entire system is derived. The system stability is then analyzed using the impedance-based analysis method. To address the instability issues, a suppression method based on a single feedback parameter k is proposed for FSC-VSPSU. This method reshapes the equivalent impedance with minimal complexity, eliminating the need for filters that typically degrade dynamic response performance in existing solutions. Simulation and experimental results are presented to validate the effectiveness of the established impedance model and the proposed control strategy.

item.title: Optimal Planning of Battery Storage to Enhance RES Integration and Frequency Security: A Planning-Validation-Adjustment Framework
item.description: The integration of a high proportion of renewable energy sources (RES) poses severe challenges to the flexibility and frequency stability of power systems. More battery storage systems (BES) are essential for supporting RES integration, with the fast frequency response (FFR) of grid-forming BES being particularly crucial for frequency stability. However, incorporating frequency dynamics into planning models is challenging due to the complexity and difficulty in model tractability. To address these issues, a two-level “Planning-Validation-Adjustment” framework is proposed for grid-forming BES planning. The system frequency response model is integrated into the conventional planning model, and a frequency-dynamics feasibility-driven Benders-cut mechanism is developed to iteratively adjust BES planning decisions based on the frequency response feedback. Specifically, the upper-level planning model optimizes the allocation and sizing of grid-forming BES under RES uncertainty scenarios. In the lower level, the FFR of grid-forming BES is incorporated into robust dynamic frequency constraints to ensure safety margins under uncertain power disturbances. When frequency violations are identified in the lower-level model, corresponding Benders-cuts are generated and fed back to the upper level to update planning decisions. To efficiently solve the proposed model, a Benders-Cut-based iterative solution algorithm embedded with progressive hedging algorithm (PHA) is developed. Simulation studies on the modified IEEE 39-bus system confirm the effectiveness of the proposed planning framework and solution approach.

item.title: Stability Boundary Analysis for Grid-Following and Grid-Forming Inverters
item.description: Studying the dynamics and stability of inverter-driven power systems has become a top priority for grid operators in view of the massive renewable energy deployment. The simplest and most widely used model for stability analysis for such networks is the single-inverter single-bus (SISB) equivalent model. Although quite insightful, SISB models with an L-filter for the inverter suffer from implementation difficulties due to algebraic loops. This paper develops a closed-form SISB model for grid-following (GFL) and grid-forming (GFM) inverters and derives symbolic formulation for the linearized system, which resolves algebraic loops and obtains fast results with uncompromised precision. This model is then used in an N-Dimensional Stability Boundary Tracking (ND-SBT) algorithm, which identifies the stability boundary for GFL and GFM inverters. This is the first method to infer the stability region for an arbitrary number of dimensions in a computationally efficient and software-agnostic manner while preserving accuracy. A detailed sensitivity analysis yields useful findings on the stability impact of individual parameters, while the results are validated using the IEEE 14-bus system.

item.title: Voltage Deviation and Wideband Oscillation Constrained Stability and Weakness Analysis of Hybrid GFM/GFL Multi-Converter Systems
item.description: The oscillation risk assessment and oscillation source identification pose significant challenges for heterogeneous multi-converter systems such as hybrid grid-forming and grid-following renewable energy station (GFM/GFL-RES). Furthermore, the theoretical impact of GFM converter capacity and placement on the voltage support strength and resonant modes of each bus remains unexplored. To fill this gap, this paper proposes a stability assessment and weakness location method for hybrid GFM/GFL multi-converter systems. Firstly, a power flow method incorporating the reactive power support capability of the GFM converter and the multiple renewable short-circuit ratio is used to evaluate the voltage support strength at each bus. Then, a s-domain modal analysis method incorporating frequency-coupling is proposed. This method accurately extracts system resonant modes by combining modal impedance curves and a center-difference Newton-Raphson iterative algorithm. Finally, the steady-state stability region is established by integrating steady-state voltage stability and small-signal stability. The method in this paper is validated through a 16-converter 34-bus GFM/GFL-RES, and the influence of GFM capacity and placement on the steady-state stability region of the system is quantified. The accuracy of the proposed stability analysis method is verified by time-domain simulation.

item.title: A Novel Hybrid DC/DC Converter With Residual DC Fault Current Blocking Capability for All-DC Offshore Wind Farms
item.description: All-DC offshore wind farms have received extensive attention in recent years, but DC fault protection poses a significant challenge for their development. This paper proposes a novel hybrid DC/DC converter with fault current blocking circuit, which is capable of quickly suppressing the residual DC fault current to enable fast DC fault isolation using DC switches (DCSs). The fault clearance time is significantly reduced from around 500 ms to less than 14 ms, yielding quick system recovery. The operating principle of the proposed topology during normal operation and DC faults is elaborated and an enhanced power sharing control is also proposed to achieve DC voltage and current sharing among the modules of the proposed topology. In addition, the DC reactance of the proposed topology is significantly reduced by using interleaved control. The presented simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed hybrid DC/DC converter and its control scheme in blocking circulating fault currents and facilitating DC fault ride-through using DCSs.

item.title: Improved Reachability Analysis for VSC-MTDC Integrated Offshore Wind Farms Based on Adaptive Parameter Optimization
item.description: To address the challenges presented by the complex nonlinear dynamics and limited disturbance resilience of offshore wind farms (OWFs) integrated via voltage source converter based multi-terminal high voltage direct current (VSC-MTDC) system under large disturbances, an improved reachability analysis method is proposed to capture dynamic behaviors of the system. The analysis framework encompasses: 1) high-dimensional nonlinear differential and hybrid models of VSC-MTDC integrated OWFs system are constructed based on Kron reduction method to enhance computational efficiency and mitigate the limitations imposed by algebraic constraints; 2) an adaptive time-step optimization algorithm is implemented to further improve computational efficiency while enhancing the capability to capture both transient and steady-state dynamics of hybrid system; 3) the improved reachability algorithm is designed to quantitatively analyze the operational states of VSC-MTDC integrated OWFs system under uncertainties and varying operating conditions. Case studies on VSC-MTDC integrated OWFs system are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in revealing complex nonlinear characteristics and safety verification under large disturbances.

item.title: Accurate and Computationally Efficient Aggregated Modeling of Offshore Wind Farms for Grid Compliance
item.description: Detailed electromagnetic transient models of offshore wind farms incorporate the individual wind turbine dynamics and controls of many wind turbines (WTs), resulting in substantial computational burdens. This limits their practicality for large-scale system studies. While aggregation techniques reduce this burden, they often compromise accuracy, particularly in capturing dynamic behaviour during power system disturbances. To overcome this challenge, this paper proposes a gaussian mixture model clustering with information-theoretic averaging to group similar WTs and derive representative aggregated models. A structured eigenvalue sensitivity analysis is conducted to identify the control parameters that have the greatest impact on system dynamics, reducing the dimensionality of the parameter space. Additionally, an improved multi-objective gradient descent optimisation strategy is developed to identify and provide optimal values of system and control parameters under varying wind conditions and fault scenarios to ensure accuracy. The proposed modeling applicability is further demonstrated through assessment on an HVDC connected offshore wind farm under large disturbance and weak grid conditions in the time and frequency domains. Finally, the aggregated model's performance is validated against the detailed benchmark to ensure U.K.’s grid compliance on modeling, confirming its ability to replicate key dynamic and steady-state characteristics with substantially reduced computational effort.

item.title: Unified Transmission-Distribution Coordination for Secure and Economical Frequency Support From Distributed Energy Resources
item.description: Utilizing numerous distributed energy resources (DERs) for frequency regulation is a promising pathway to enhance the security and economy offuture power systems. However, a fundamental challenge arises from the emergent collective behavior of these resources: their aggregated response, intended to support macro-level system frequency, can jeopardize micro-level local voltage security within distribution networks. To this end, this paper proposes a unified hierarchical framework based on the principle of synergistic co-design. The framework employs a stage-dependent architecture that systematically integrates distinct control strategies for different regulation stages. For security-critical response, it utilizes a novel stage-aware adaptive virtual synchronous generator (VSG) control method with stability formally proven by Lyapunov analysis, while for economic efficiency, it transitions to a distributed control method for cost-optimal restoration. The entire process is underpinned by a proactive voltage security management strategy that enforces micro-level safety. To enable efficient analysis of these complex dynamics, a dual time-scale information interaction mechanism is designed to accelerate the transmission-distribution (T&D) co-simulation process. Simulations on a hybrid transmission-distribution test system validate the framework's effectiveness, demonstrating that the proposed approach enables scalable, secure, and economical frequency support while effectively managing the emergent voltage challenges from collective DER control.

item.title: Enhancing Offshore Wind Integration by Integrated Electricity and Gas Systems With Hydrogen Blending: The Case of Ireland
item.description: The offshore wind farm is a pivotal generation component in the decarbonization of energy systems. However, their large capacity and inherent uncertainty pose great challenges for smooth integration into current electricity systems. This paper proposes an offshore wind accommodation scheme that uses the flexibility of integrated electricity and gas systems and hydrogen blending techniques. Firstly, an uncertainty model for offshore wind farm generation is proposed, where a recursive tree-based method is used to improve the modelling efficiency of cumulative wake effects. Then, a distributionally robust chance-constrained operation scheme of hydrogen-blended integrated electricity and gas systems is proposed, to regulate the gas system safety with stochastic hydrogen injection from offshore wind via power-to-gas, as well as variant gas compositions across the gas network. Gas security is modelled as chance constraints for aligning with practical gas safety regulations. A unified affine policy, embedded with tailored sequential convex programming procedures, is developed to handle the high-order nonconvexities in the distributionally robust chance-constrained optimisation problem. Finally, the proposed method is used in the large-scale Ireland Island energy system with real network data. Our results indicate a remarkable improvement of 156.8% in offshore wind accommodation and 64.31% in carbon emission mitigation with the proposed operation strategy. This underscores the crucial role of hydrogen integration in meeting Ireland’s decarbonization ambitions by 2050.

item.title: Hybrid Score-Driven Multivariate Probabilistic Forecasting of Correlated PV Generation Using Deep Mixture Density Network
item.description: With massive integration of photovoltaic (PV) generation in power systems, multivariate probabilistic forecasting (MPF) is essential for analysis and decision-making of power systems, which provides comprehensive information about uncertainty of future PV generation. Traditional MPF methods separate probabilistic forecasting and dependency structure estimation, limiting their ability to generate multivariate distributions in an analytical form. This paper innovatively proposes a hybrid score-driven MPF method to directly generate semi-parametric joint predictive density of correlated PV generation using deep mixture density network. Firstly, an integrated framework of MPF based on multivariate Gaussian mixture models is established to unify the training of probabilistic forecasting and the optimization of conditional correlation modeling. Then, a novel hybrid score-based network loss function with the closed-form expression is meticulously devised for the robust training of MPF. In addition, graph structure learning is incorporated to adaptively capture spatial dependency among correlated PV generation. To address overwhelming parameters of joint distributions, a parameter-reduced structure of covariance matrices using incomplete Cholesky decomposition is formulated, which enhances the tractability of MPF in high-dimensional settings. Comprehensive case studies on three realistic PV power datasets are conducted to validate the superior performance of the proposed method for day-ahead multivariate probabilistic forecasting.

item.title: Data-Driven Microgrid Operation Toward Optimized Battery Energy Storage Lifetime Degradation
item.description: This paper proposes a new data-driven approach for two-stage operation of a microgrid (MG) toward optimized battery energy storage (BES) lifetime degradation. At the first stage (day-ahead), the BES degradation range over the scheduled day is optimally allocated under day-ahead forecast of random variables. To accurately quantify BES degradation under temperature impact, a convex BES electrothermal-aging model is constructed. Then, a dynamic model constraint coefficient determination method is proposed for optimizing BES lifetime degradation using the historical operation and updated current information. At the second stage (real-time), an approximate dynamic programming method is developed to real-time optimize the MG operation after multiple uncertainties are realized. Considering the temperature dependent constraints of BES, a sequential decomposed value function slope update method is designed for enhanced optimization feasibility, BES aging quantification accuracy, and training efficacy, which can deliver high-quality solutions under uncertainties. Case studies on a MG system demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

item.title: Distributed Large-Signal Stability Analysis and Stabilization for DC Microgrid Clusters
item.description: DC microgrid clusters (DCMGCs) enhance resilience and efficiency but face significant large-signal stability challenges due to growing integration of distributed energy sources and power electronics, exacerbated by inter-subgrid couplings that remain poorly understood. This work proposes a novel distributed approach for large-signal stability analysis and stabilization of DCMGCs. The core methodology focuses on individual DC microgrids and their coupling interactions. With the decomposition-aggregation fashion, scalar Lyapunov functions are employed to derive distributed stability criteria, and two metrics (stability strength and connectivity strength) and the associated decoupling ratio are introduced to explicitly quantify the impact of coupling dynamics on system stability. Based on the identified relationships between power flow control coefficients and system stability, key parameters are optimally designed to ensure system-wide stable operation. Compared to centralized methods requiring global states, this approach reduces modeling complexity, enhances scalability, and lowers conservatism. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated through numerical and hardware-in-the-loop experiments.

item.title: TD3-Enhanced Adaptive Active Disturbance Rejection Control for Bidirectional DC--DC Converter in Photovoltaic-Battery Hybrid Microgrids
item.description: This paper addresses the stability and reliability issues associated with the application of linear active disturbance rejection control (LADRC) in hybrid PV-battery microgrids. A hierarchical control strategy tailored for bidirectional DC-DC converter in the system is proposed. The strategy comprises an outer voltage regulation loop implemented with an enhanced LADRC and an inner current tracking loop driven by a proportional-integral (PI) controller. Specifically, the traditional LADRC is enhanced via an improved linear extended state observer, which achieves separate compensation for low-order and high-order disturbances. Additionally, the observer parameters are optimized in real time based on the Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm. Finally, simulations and experiments under multiple operating conditions are conducted to validate the proposed control strategy, demonstrating its superior performance over double closed-loop PI control and traditional LADRC.

item.title: Distributed Adaptive Unified Fault-Tolerant Control Scheme for Multiple VSGs in Islanded Microgrids
item.description: Sensor and actuator faults are an inherent challenge in islanded microgrids, often leading to performance degradation and threatening system stability in the absence of robust fault-tolerant mechanisms. This paper proposes a novel distributed adaptive unified fault-tolerant control (FTC) scheme to address these issues in multi-virtual synchronous generator (VSG) microgrids. The proposed methodology begins with a state estimation technique, employing two third-order observers and system transmission characteristics. This approach enables accurate estimation of key electrical quantities, such as active power, angular frequency, and output voltage, while effectively suppressing unknown disturbances. These estimations facilitate the construction of a linear state-space model, forming the foundation for fault-tolerant control. A key innovation of this work lies in the unified modeling of sensor and actuator faults. By analyzing the control dynamics and physical mechanisms of microgrids, the equivalence between sensor and actuator faults is established, leading to a comprehensive fault representation framework. Building upon this model, a distributed adaptive FTC strategy is developed to improve the performance of voltage, frequency and inertia support, as well as the power sharing accuracy, under unknown fault conditions. The scheme integrates information from both local and neighboring VSGs, enhancing fault resilience and system reliability. Extensive simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed FTC scheme, demonstrating its capability to maintain stable operation in islanded multi-VSG microgrids subject to sensor and actuator faults.

item.title: CarbonGPT: Meta Causal Graph-Enhanced Large Language Models for Carbon Emission Forecasting in Large-Scale Power Distribution Networks
item.description: Carbon emission reduction has emerged as a global core development objective, where dynamic carbon flow perception serves as the critical foundation for low-carbon dispatch. However, the electrification shift of transportation carbon emissions caused by large-scale electric vehicle (EV) grid integration, coupled with the grid impact from renewable energy source (RES) volatility, poses unprecedented challenges for accurate carbon emission prediction. Although adaptive graphs and large language models (LLMs) can achieve carbon emission prediction for power distribution networks (PDNs) under data sparsity scenarios, the spurious cross-variable correlations derived from their adaptive topologies tend to be amplified by LLMs, consequently constraining model learning and reasoning capabilities. To address this, we propose a novel model named CarbonGPT. This innovative model employs a causal encoder to uncover genuine cross-variable causal relationships, while incorporating a meta causal graph dictionary and lightweight alignment to enhance the comprehension of carbon feature representations by LLMs in EV and RES grid integration scenarios. Extensive simulations in PDNs under large-scale integration of EVs and RESs have demonstrated that CarbonGPT consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance in both prediction accuracy and effectiveness. Codes are available at https://github.com/lishijie15/CarbonGPT.

item.title: Real-Time AI-Driven Energy and Flexibility Management for a Technical Virtual Power Plant
item.description: This paper presents an AI-powered framework for real-time energy and flexibility self-scheduling of a technical virtual power plant (TVPP). The TVPP integrates electric vehicle charging stations (EVCSs), energy storage systems (ESSs), photovoltaic (PV) panels, wind turbines (WTs), and distributed generators (DGs) to participate in wholesale energy and flexibility markets while adhering to distribution network constraints. The framework employs a two-stage approach to maximize profitability. In the first stage, a deep learning model forecasts short-term flexibility requirements. In the second stage, optimization is divided into two interdependent sub-problems. The first sub-problem coordinates EVCS and ESS operations through multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL), using multi-agent proximal policy optimization (MAPPO) with centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE). The second, formulated as a linear programming (LP) problem, addresses the remaining system-level operations and constraints. These sub-problems interact iteratively through a feedback mechanism to align local decisions with global objectives. To enhance training efficiency, single-agent policies are pre-trained using proximal policy optimization (PPO) in simplified environments and later fine-tuned via transfer learning in the full MADRL framework. The proposed method is evaluated on the IEEE-33 bus test system, demonstrating superior operational efficiency and profitability over existing approaches.

item.title: Novel Decentralized Load Shedding Algorithm for Preserving Voltage Stability in Smart Grids
item.description: The occurrence of a short-circuit fault leads to the voltage reduction in power networks, and subsequently, induction motors experience relative speed decrease. This speed decline can sometimes lead to motor stalling for which, motors demand several times their rated current, drawing considerable reactive current. In the networks with a high penetration of motor loads, this can delay voltage recovery, posing a risk of short-term voltage instability. In order to enhance stability, this paper proposes a load shedding index based on kinetic energy to determine the level of kinetic energy of each motor. Motor loads, proportional to their kinetic energy, impose stress on the network. Thus, a load shedding scheme based on this index is presented. Initially, the proposed index is evaluated experimentally for an industrial induction motor and then is assessed on two active distribution networks. Subsequently, to evaluate the comprehensiveness of the index for larger networks, proper appraisal is conducted on a real sizable, multi-voltage-level power grid model and compared to other load-shedding schemes. Results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in addressing short-term voltage instability, requiring less load shedding compared to other approaches. Furthermore, the proposed method is computationally efficient and suitable for real-time implementation.

item.title: A Short-Circuit Calculation Method for Distribution Networks With 100% Converter-Based Generators Based on Outer-Layer Voltage Approximation and Inner-Layer Spectral Gradient Iteration
item.description: With the continuous advancement of power electronic equipment and the widespread integration of distributed generation, distribution networks composed entirely of converter-based generators have become one of the key development approaches for low-carbon and flexible modern distribution systems. This paper investigates a short-circuit calculation method for 100% converter-based generator distribution networks with system voltage established by V/f-controlled converters and multi-point grid-following distributed generation integration. The limitations of the widely used fixed-point iteration method in short-circuit calculations for active distribution networks are analyzed. The intrinsic relationships between the capacity of V/f-controlled generators, the capacity and connection locations of grid-following distributed generators, the magnitude of fault resistance, and the convergence behavior of the fixed-point iteration sequence are revealed. To address the convergence issues of traditional iterative algorithms caused by the capacity of converter-based generators and fault conditions, a short-circuit calculation method based on outer-layer voltage approximation and inner-layer spectral gradient iteration is proposed. The global convergence of the proposed method is mathematically proven. A case study involving a 100% converter -based generator distribution network with five converter-based generator nodes is conducted. A simulation model is built in PSCAD/EMTDC to simulate fault scenarios. The effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method are validated by comparing the short-circuit calculation results with simulation results and benchmarking against fixed-point iteration methods.

item.title: Co-Optimization of Resource Allocation and Operation in Large-Scale Energy Systems---Part II: Methods and Case Studies
item.description: This two-part paper proposes a parallel-distributed equivalent projection—Monte Carlo tree search (EP-MCTS) method for allocation and operation co-optimization in large-scale energy systems. Building upon the theoretical foundation established in Part I, Part II validates the proposed method through applications in both non-hierarchical energy systems (e.g., integrated energy systems, IESs) and hierarchical systems (e.g., transmission-distribution systems, TDSs), demonstrating its efficacy in overcoming computational and privacy barriers. To address the cyclic EP problem arising in hierarchical TDS applications, a novel cycle-resolving strategy extends EP’s applicability beyond tree-like EP problems to cyclic EP problems while preserving solution equivalence. Case studies demonstrate our method’s intrinsic privacy protection by compressing sensitive data into non-reconstructable numerical proxies, reducing shared parameters to merely 0.548% of benchmark requirements in IES applications. The proposed EP-MCTS also enables computational tractability for previously unsolvable large-scale problems (11,772-node and 150-scenario cases solved within 120 hours), accelerating initial feasible solutions by $8.09\times $ with higher accuracy and achieving $34.49\times $ faster convergence to satisfactory solutions through variable reduction (up to 98.78% fewer decision variables) and parallelized MCTS processing.

item.title: Dynamic Hydrogen Injection in Integrated Electricity-Gas Systems: A PDEs-Embedded Flexible Operation Strategy
item.description: Integrated electricity-gas systems (IEGS) with hydrogen injection has emerged as a crucial pathway to decarbonize energy systems. By coordinating the flexibility of the gas system and hydrogen blending, the accommodation of large-scale renewable energy integrated in power systems can be further improved. However, the time-varying nature of dynamic hydrogen injection, influenced by stochastic renewable energy, can lead to fluctuations in gas concentrations in the gas network, threatening the secure operation of IEGS. This paper focuses on the low-carbon flexible operation strategy for IEGS with dynamic hydrogen injection. First, a flexible operation strategy of hydrogen-mixed gas turbine is developed with a detailed correlation characterization of carbon emission, combustion thermoldynamics, and chemical reaction kinetics under dynamic hydrogen ratios. Second, an optimal IEGS dispatch strategy is formulated in which the joint dynamics of the system component and the gas flow is captured to accurately track the time-varying concentrations of hydrogen in the gas network. Third, a set of discretized partial differential equations (PDEs) is utilized to model mixed gas flows, leading to a PDEs-constrained optimization model. Finally, Taylor series expansions for PDE linearization and McCormick envelope for bilinear terms are employed, enabling tractable algebraic representations. A sequential linear programming (SLP) algorithm with adaptive penalty factors is developed to drive relaxation tightening more efficiently. Numerical results on a 24-bus-20-node system and a practical 197-bus-171-node system in Northwest China illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed model.

item.title: Triple-Stage Collaborative Optimization Method for Cross-Regional Data Centers Considering Optimal Coalition Matching
item.description: With the rapid advancements of artificial intelligence, cross-regional collaboration among data centers (DCs) has emerged as an effective strategy to enhance resource utilization and reduce energy consumption. Without such collaboration, DCs may experience higher operational costs and energy consumption due to imbalances in computational load and disparities in regional electricity prices. Therefore, a triple-stage collaborative optimization method for cross-regional DCs considering optimal coalition matching is proposed. In Stage 1, a potential coalition search method for DCs is proposed, and the Bron-Kerbosch pivoting algorithm is employed to identify high-quality potential coalitions under multi-dimensional complementarity. Then, an optimal collaborative operation model for DCs incorporating fine-grained cooling system modeling is established in Stage 2, aiming to jointly optimize computational load scheduling and energy management. Based on this, an optimal dynamic coalition matching mechanism for DCs is proposed in Stage 3, integrating a rolling-horizon control strategy to adaptively update coalition structures and operation strategies under varying fluctuation levels. Case studies on ten heterogeneous cross-regional DCs demonstrate that the proposed method effectively reduces energy consumption, increases coalition profits and member satisfaction, thereby enhancing both economic performance and operational stability.

item.title: Coordinated Scheduling of Multiple Frequency Services in Electricity-Gas-Hydrogen Systems Based on Federated Warm Starts
item.description: The high penetration of renewable energy and variable load profiles in modern power systems call for enhanced frequency security in the event of large and sudden power disturbances. Integrated energy systems, with the introduction of external energy networks, hold strong potential for mitigating frequency deviations. This paper proposes a coordinated scheduling framework of multiple frequency services in an electricity-gas-hydrogen coupled integrated energy system based on federated warm starts. On top of the scheduling model of a closed-loop electricity-gas-hydrogen system, we formulate the set of frequency security constraints as linear/bilinear constraints, where generators and electrolyzers with heterogeneous frequency support capabilities collectively contribute to enhanced/primary frequency control. In terms of the bilinear term in the reconfigured nadir frequency constraint, we propose a SplitNN-based federated learning framework to allow the energy systems to collectively generate warm starts for the bilinear variables in a privacy-preserving manner, with which the McCormick envelope can be significantly tightened. Case studies show that the electrolyzers in the hydrogen system can significantly mitigate frequency deviations, while the proposed warm start approach achieves a higher accuracy and computational efficiency.

item.title: Probabilistic Energy Flow Calculation Based on Data-Driven Arbitrary Polynomial Chaos Expansion With Mixed Uncertainties
item.description: Probabilistic energy flow (PEF) analysis is an essential tool for assessing the impact of uncertainties on the steady-state operation of integrated electricity-gas systems (IEGS). Existing PEF studies primarily focus on the aleatory uncertainties in input variables, which are commonly represented by precise probability distributions. However, epistemic uncertainties arising from limited data and the resulting incomplete knowledge are often neglected, potentially leading to bias in risk assessment. Moreover, computationally efficient PEF analysis remains challenging when a large number of uncertain inputs are involved, due to the high computational burden of conventional methods. To address these issues, this paper proposes a data-driven PEF method for IEGS under mixed aleatory and epistemic uncertainties. An interval probability model based on bootstrap sampling is developed to characterize the uncertainties without strong prior assumptions. On this basis, arbitrary polynomial chaos (aPC) is employed to propagate uncertainties using the generated datasets. To alleviate its dimensionality issue, a compressive sensing model based on ${l} _{1}$ - ${l} _{2}$ minimization is adopted to construct a sparse aPC representation. Moreover, an adaptive sparse strategy is further developed to determine an appropriate sparsity level considering the different nonlinear properties of subsystems. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method improves the computational efficiency of PEF analysis, while maintaining accurate risk assessment.

item.title: Machine Learning-Based Long-Term Forecasting of Load Composition Considering Exogenous Variables
item.description: Long-term load forecasting (LTLF) plays a key role in the planning and operation of power systems, enabling utilities to anticipate demand patterns and ensure grid stability. This paper presents a novel approach to LTLF by exploring the influence of various exogenous variables not only on load forecasting accuracy at grid supply point (GSP) but also on the accuracy of forecasting the composition of the load. Using a bidirectional gated-recurrent unit (GRU) neural network, the study integrates macroeconomic factors, weather conditions, and social dynamics as key exogenous factors in the 1-year-ahead active and reactive load forecasts. Then, with a feed-forward neural network, the forecasted load is disaggregated into load categories for every day of the year. Three GSPs from a real distribution network with varying gross domestic product (GDP) and mean flow characteristics serve as case studies to validate the proposed model. Results of the first stage indicate that incorporating exogenous variables enhances forecasting performance, with population demonstrating the strongest correlation for these particular datasets. The results of the second stage demonstrate that the forecasted load can be decomposed into constituent categories accurately enough and that load decomposition is not very sensitive to errors in the forecast. The decomposed load forecast results were validated with a Household Electricity Survey study performed by the UK government.

item.title: Grid-Interactive and Dual-Side Demand Responsive Timetable Scheduling Optimization for Flexible Urban Metro Systems
item.description: Metro systems, as major electricity consumers, establish a critical demand-side linkage between public transportation services and the utility power grid. With ongoing expansion, metro systems impose increasing challenges on electricity supply due to their concentrated energy demands, while also offering significant potential as controllable loads to enhance grid flexibility. To enhance grid flexibility while effectively meeting passenger demand, this paper proposes a novel dual-side demand-responsive and grid-interactive metro operation framework. This framework allows metro systems to support grid-side demand response by aligning operational schedules with grid conditions and passenger demand, without compromising service quality. A spatiotemporal model is established to estimate metro train power consumption under various speed profiles and real-time passenger loads. An adaptive iterative greedy algorithm is developed to coordinate metro operations in response to real-time power system conditions. Unlike global optimization approaches that suffer from exponential complexity, this algorithm employs a sequential and simulation-based decision process. It prioritizes real-time feasibility by making locally optimal dispatching decisions, thereby achieving a practical trade-off between solution optimality and computational speed. The algorithm offers high computational efficiency and dynamically optimizes train timetables and operational modes, thereby improving grid flexibility. Case studies using real-world metro data demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a 17.8% reduction in electricity costs and provides up to 6.56 MW of DR capacity without compromising passenger service. This study reveals the potential of metro systems as large-scale, dispatchable loads that contribute to the flexibility of utility grids.

item.title: Convex-Hull of Locational Marginal Carbon Emission for Carbon Management Considering Emissions During Non-Steady-State Operation Hours
item.description: The increasing penetration of renewable energy, while essential for decarbonization, can intensify thermal-unit cycling and ramping, aggravate network congestion, and increase emissions during non-steady-state operating periods (i.e., start-ups, shutdowns, and fast ramping). Research indicates that carbon emissions during such hours could increase to 2–6 times those of steady-state operation hours. However, conventional studies often ignore the impact of intertemporal constraints on carbon measurements, leading to significant underestimation of carbon emission. To address this gap, this paper extends the locational marginal carbon emission (LMCE) framework by incorporating non-steady-state hours emissions through a convex-hull-based variant of LMCE (i.e., LMCE2) model.
